text:
## Time-of-use tariff design
Time-of-use tariffs with a 3x to 5x ratio between peak and off-peak rates motivate consumers to shift flexible loads into midday solar surplus periods. Effective shift is 5-15 percent of residential and 10-25 percent of commercial demand.

## Flexible loads suitable for solar-hour shifting
Water heating, HVAC precooling, EV charging, irrigation pumping, and industrial batch processes are good candidates for shifting into midday high-solar hours. Automated controls achieve 2-4x higher shift than manual programs.

## EV charging alignment
Workplace EV charging naturally aligns with midday solar. Managed charging programs can defer 60-80 percent of daytime EV load to align with local solar output.

## Industrial flexibility
Industries such as aluminum smelting, chlor-alkali, and data centers can modulate 5-20 percent of load on sub-hourly timescales. Bilateral demand response contracts monetize this without impacting production.

## Forecast-informed demand signals
Publishing day-ahead solar forecasts to demand-response aggregators 24 hours in advance improves participation rates by 30-50 percent versus same-day dispatch signals.

## Building thermal mass as storage
Precooling commercial buildings by 1-2 degrees Celsius during high-solar midday hours shifts 20-40 percent of afternoon cooling load off the grid without comfort impact.

## Ramp-rate management
Grid operators typically limit plant ramp rates to 10 percent of nameplate capacity per minute to preserve frequency stability. When solar output drops sharply due to passing clouds, fast-start gas peakers or battery inverters must cover the shortfall within seconds.

## Reserve scheduling
Operators hold spinning reserves of at least 3 percent of forecast solar output during daytime hours to absorb forecast error. If the day-ahead forecast shows low confidence, reserves are scaled up to 5-7 percent.

## Forecast-informed dispatch
Economic dispatch windows of 5 to 15 minutes allow operators to re-balance generation as short-term solar forecasts refine. Intraday market participation reduces curtailment by up to 30 percent on variable-irradiance days.

## Frequency response
Solar inverters with grid-forming capability can provide synthetic inertia and fast frequency response, replacing functions traditionally handled by synchronous generators. Required droop setting is typically 4-5 percent.

## Curtailment as last resort
Curtailment of solar is used when transmission is congested or reserves are exhausted. Well-managed systems keep curtailment below 5 percent of annual solar generation.

## Geographic and technology diversification
Combining solar from multiple sites 50-100 km apart smooths aggregate output by 20-40 percent versus a single site, because cloud transients rarely correlate across that distance. Pairing solar with wind further reduces variability.

## Interconnection requirements
Grid codes for utility-scale solar require low-voltage ride-through (LVRT), reactive power control, and remote telemetry. Plants above 5 MW typically must provide 15-minute real-time output telemetry to the system operator.

## Hosting capacity analysis
Feeders have a solar hosting capacity determined by thermal limits, voltage rise, and protection coordination. Exceeding hosting capacity without mitigation causes voltage excursions above 1.05 per unit. Advanced inverter functions (volt-var, volt-watt) can raise hosting capacity by 30-60 percent.

## Flexibility options ranking
The IEA ranks flexibility options by cost: demand response (lowest) < storage (short duration) < dispatchable thermal (mid duration) < long-duration storage and hydrogen (highest). Deploy in that order for least-cost integration.

## Curtailment vs storage economics
Below roughly 15 percent annual curtailment, economic curtailment is typically cheaper than adding storage. Above 15 percent, storage is more cost-effective.

## Sizing guidance
A practical battery sizing heuristic is 2 to 4 hours of storage at 50-80 percent of plant peak output. For a 1 MW peak solar plant, 0.5-0.8 MW / 2-4 MWh is a typical utility-scale pairing.

## Charge window optimization
Charging is most efficient between 10:00 and 14:00 local time when irradiance is high and the plant would otherwise be exporting excess energy. Avoid charging past 80 percent state-of-charge to extend cycle life.

## Discharge scheduling
Discharge the battery during the evening peak (17:00-21:00 typically) to shift solar energy to high-demand hours. This captures the highest time-of-use tariff differential and reduces grid stress during the duck curve ramp.

## Round-trip efficiency
Modern lithium-ion BESS achieve 85-92 percent round-trip efficiency. Factor efficiency losses into economic dispatch: a 90 percent RTE means 10 percent of stored solar is lost, which should be priced against direct export revenue.

## Cycle life and depth of discharge
Limiting depth of discharge to 80 percent roughly doubles cycle life compared to 100 percent DoD. Over a 10-year horizon this can reduce LCOE of storage by 15-25 percent.

## State of charge buffer for forecast error
Maintain a 10-15 percent SoC buffer against next-day solar forecast uncertainty to ensure discharge commitments can be met on low-irradiance days.
